feedback.text: This feedback piece on the DFA is a collaborative statement from the Tech Policy Youth Committee (TPYC) a Copenhagen-based youth association uniting ambitious students passionate about tech policy development from a multidisciplinary perspective. At the TPYC, we are optimistic about the forthcoming DFA, as it raises important points on digital consumer protection. We were especially thrilled to see the emphasis on the video game industry e.g. as described in the fitness check working document (European Commission, 2024). On that note, the TPYC has a few brief points on what we find important to consider when developing policy related to loot boxes. First of all, we encourage careful consideration of the fact that loot boxes come in different types that might be problematic for different reasons. While simple, the embedded-isolated framework from Nielsen and Grabarczyk (2019) is useful here. In short, it can be used to determine whether real money is involved (embedded) or if it solely relates to in-game currencies and prizes (isolated) for respectively the eligibility conditions and the reward which results in four types of loot boxes. Cerulli-Harms et al. (2020), a study requested by the European Parliament, draws on this classification as well. As of now in most countries, including Denmark, only embedded-embedded loot boxes with real money as input and output are considered and regulated as gambling, as seen in e.g. CounterStrike: Global Offensive. However, the TPYC strongly encourages the DFA to view embedded-isolated loot boxes where real money is the input, but cannot be withdrawn as equally gambling-like. This includes the card pack in the Ultimate Team mode of the FC games (f.k.a. FIFA). This recommendation is in line with those found in Xiao (2022). It is essential not to neglect this type of loot box as less problematic than the embedded-embedded type. While the reward cannot be converted to real money, one must remember that having a highly rated player in FC or a rare skin in CS:GO likely has more value to a gamer especially younger ones. Many invest substantial time, money, and mental energy in pursuing these rewards through randomized, gamblified systems, that can have negative effect on especially children engaging in purchasing loot boxes even though the reward itself is of no monetary value. Regulation on loot boxes must acknowledge social prestige and virtual community rather than reduce it to a matter of spending and earning money. As you mention in the fitness check document, in-game purchases with real money is a huge, if not the largest part, of game companies revenues, and these numbers are growing (European Commission, 2024, pp. 20-21). Only by forcing regulation on this type of loot box, we can have some control of their exposure of gambling-like elements to adolescents and other vulnerable groups. Secondly, it is essential that online videogames that are played transnationally are regulated by EU legislation, which is why we are in favour of including it in the DFA. Having individual national policy is meaningless and might end up making it less enjoyable to play online games, where the goal of the DFA is the opposite. E.g., Denoo et al. (2023) describes how the Belgian ban on premium currencies led to major competitive disadvantage when facing opponents from other countries online in FIFA Ultimate Team, since they were no longer able to pay to win like the others, making it impossible to get as good teams. It is therefore also crucial, that the DFA will be a regulation that has direct, binding force in all member states. If it will be a directive, it will be up to the individual countries to devise their own laws on how to reach these goals, which can lead to the same problem as seen in the Belgian ban. In the TPYC, we are very willing to discuss further and knowledge-share on this topic. We can be reached at cphtpyc@pm.me.
